title: Higher Order Sumsets in Sets of Positive Density
speaker: Florian K Richter
abstract: In this talk we present a natural generalization of a sumset conjecture of Erdos to higher orders, asserting that every subset of the integers with positive density contains a sumset $B_1+\ldots +B_k$ where $B_1, \ldots , B_k$ are infinite. Our method relies on a newfound connection between k-fold sumsets in the integers and return times of orbits in k-fold joinings of measure preserving systems arising from the Host-Kra structure theory. This talk is based on joint work with Kra, Moreira, and Robertson.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Positive-density higher-order sumset target
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the goal of proving that every positive-density subset of the natural numbers contains a k-fold sumset of infinite sets, and frames the proof as a higher-order analogue of the k=2 problem with nilsystem parallels to Roth and Szemerédi theory.

proof_stages.label: Furstenberg correspondence model
proof_stages.summary: A positive-density set A is converted into a transitive topological dynamical system (X,T), a distinguished point a, a set E, and an invariant probability measure with positive mass on E, so that membership in A is encoded by return times of a to E.

proof_stages.label: Erdős cubes as the dynamical form of sumsets
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines binary cube spaces and Erdős lines, squares, and k-cubes by requiring each lower face to accumulate on the corresponding upper face, then proves that an Erdős k-cube whose terminal point lies in a neighborhood forces the return-time set to contain a k-fold sumset.

proof_stages.label: Main dynamical theorem and combinatorial reduction
proof_stages.summary: The central dynamical theorem asserts that, for the correspondence system and every positive-measure set E, there is an Erdős k-cube with first coordinate equal to the distinguished transitive point a and terminal coordinate in E, which together with the correspondence principle and the Erdős-cube proposition proves the original sumset theorem.

proof_stages.label: Host--Kra dynamical cubes and cubic measures
proof_stages.summary: Known dynamical cube spaces Q_k and their inductively defined cubic measures μ_k provide an abundant supply of Erdős cubes, since μ_k-almost every dynamical cube is an Erdős cube by a direct condition plus cube-coordinate symmetries, making the terminal-in-E condition easy.

proof_stages.label: Fiber measures fixing the first coordinate
proof_stages.summary: To enforce the hard condition that the first coordinate is the prescribed point a, the proof tries to condition or project the cubic measure onto the fiber over a via recursively defined measures σ_a^k, but this requires using ergodic decompositions on sets where the usual almost-everywhere disintegration is not defined.

proof_stages.label: Continuous ergodic decomposition via nilfactors
proof_stages.summary: The technical obstacle is resolved by arranging continuous ergodic decompositions, controlled by the k-step nilfactor and by a correspondence model whose factor map to that nilfactor is continuous, so that the fiber measures are defined everywhere, inherit the needed symmetries, and complete the dynamical theorem.

examples.name: Roth--Szemerédi arithmetic-progression analogy
examples.note: Roth's theorem for 3-term progressions and Szemerédi's theorem for longer progressions are used as an analogy for why higher-order sumsets should be controlled by higher-step nilsystems, rather than as a source of a new proof step in the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: the Roth/Szemerédi arithmetic-progression connection is invoked as a vague analogy about nilsystems controlling configurations, without carrying out a concrete AP computation or proof on it

passes.reason: Arithmetic progressions are regular Szemerédi configurations used only as an analogy for the sumset problem.

examples.name: Periodic parity eigenfunction versus long-block parity switching
examples.note: The contrast between the continuous eigenfunction sequence (-1)^n and a sequence that switches parity only after long blocks makes visible the idea that noncontinuous eigenfunctions can be handled by restricting to long windows where they look continuous.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely contrasts the periodic parity sequence with long-block parity switching, identifying continuous versus noncontinuous eigenfunction behavior and explaining the block-restriction trick

passes.reason: The long-block parity-switching sequence illustrates failure of continuity despite looking locally like the 2-periodic parity eigenfunction.
The period-two parity eigenfunction is a regular model, while the long-block parity-switching version is used as the noncontinuous obstruction to automatic continuity.
the parity eigenfunction is a highly regular 2-periodic sequence, while the long-block switching version is used to show global continuity of eigenfunctions/factors can fail
The long-block parity-switching sequence is contrasted with the perfectly periodic parity eigenfunction to show global continuity can fail even when local windows look periodic.
The periodic parity sequence is very regular, while the long-block switching variant is used as a noncontinuous bad case showing continuity is not automatic.

The long-block parity-switching sequence is used to exhibit the obstruction of a non-continuous eigenfunction, contrasted with the perfectly periodic parity sequence.
